ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for condensed-milk-topped French breadstick; mapped to the nearest parent class 'bread food product' (FOODON_03315873) as an approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for condensed-milk-topped soft French breadstick; A000E (Bread and similar products) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept maps specifically to this preparation; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one breadstick (approximately 150 g), as commonly sold at Singaporean café and toast stalls.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — French bread (SR Legacy #18033) and Sweetened condensed milk (SR Legacy #01009), combined in estimated ratio 60:40 by weight with butter adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for condensed-milk-topped soft French breadstick as sold in Singapore. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: French bread (FDC ID 325871) and sweetened condensed milk (FDC ID 172185), blended in an approximate 60:40 ratio by edible weight, with a small butter contribution. The input record's per_100g values (energy 300 kcal, protein 5.33 g, fat 12.0 g, carbohydrate 40.0 g, sugar 23.33 g, sodium 200 mg) are broadly consistent with this blended estimate and have been retained with minor rounding. Singapore HPB Foodo database was also consulted but does not carry a specific entry for this preparation; USDA SR Legacy is used as the primary approved source.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; white bread / baguette analogous entry
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for condensed-milk-topped soft French breadstick. GI of 72 is taken from the peer-reviewed Foster-Powell et al. (2002) international GI table entry for white French baguette (GI ~72, glucose reference). The condensed milk topping adds rapidly absorbed sugars but does not substantially alter the composite GI beyond the bread base in a mixed-food context. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 60.0) / 100 = 43.2, where carbohydrate per 150 g serving = 40.0 × 1.5 = 60.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (23.3 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (1.33 g/100 g); moderate sodium (200 mg/100 g); saturated fat 6.67 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this item: the high free-sugar load (23.3 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention and raises blood pressure independently of dietary sodium, while the saturated fat content (6.67 g/100 g) contributes to arterial stiffness; if bread is desired, choose a wholemeal variety with an unsweetened spread and limit portion to half a serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar (23.3 g/100 g), high GI (~72), high glycemic load per serving (~43.2), and low dietary fibre (1.33 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: This food is unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: the combination of a high-GI white baguette base (GI ~72), concentrated condensed milk sugar (23.3 g/100 g), and minimal fibre (1.33 g/100 g) produces a rapid and large postprandial blood glucose spike; a glycemic load of ~43.2 per standard serving far exceeds the recommended per-meal GL ceiling of 20 for glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g/100 g, trans fat 0.33 g/100 g, high sugar 23.3 g/100 g, cholesterol 20 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should avoid this food: saturated fat at 6.67 g/100 g raises LDL cholesterol, trans fat at 0.33 g/100 g is independently atherogenic even at low amounts, and the high free-sugar load (23.3 g/100 g) promotes hypertriglyceridaemia and endothelial inflammation; substituting with a wholegrain bread and a small amount of unsweetened nut butter would substantially reduce cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g/100 g exceeds 5 g/100 g threshold; trans fat 0.33 g/100 g; high sugar 23.3 g/100 g promotes pro-inflammatory cytokines
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: For individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, this food is inadvisable: saturated fat (6.67 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g inflammatory threshold, trans fat (0.33 g/100 g) is strongly pro-inflammatory, and high sugar (23.3 g/100 g) promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine activity; replace butter with extra-virgin olive oil and eliminate the condensed milk topping entirely.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Condensed Milk Soft French Breadstick is a popular Singaporean café and toast-culture snack consisting of a soft white French baguette (breadstick) that is toasted or lightly pan-fried until the exterior is golden and slightly crisp, then generously drizzled or spread with sweetened condensed milk. The result is a warm, pillowy bread with a mildly crunchy crust, intensely sweet and creamy topping, and a rich milky flavour profile. It is commonly served at kopitiams (traditional coffee shops), café chains, and toast stalls across Singapore, often alongside a cup of kopi (local coffee) or teh (tea) as a breakfast or mid-morning snack. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 300 kcal, 5.33 g protein, 12.0 g fat (of which 6.67 g is saturated), 40.0 g carbohydrate, and 23.33 g sugar — the last figure reflecting the concentrated sugar contribution of condensed milk. Dietary fibre is low at 1.33 g/100 g. The glycemic index is approximately 72 (high), yielding a glycemic load of ~43.2 per standard 150 g serving, making this food clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The high saturated fat and trans fat content also raises concern for rheumatoid arthritis management. It is vegetarian and typically halal-certified in Singapore food establishments.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific preparation. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for French bread and sweetened condensed milk in an approximate 60:40 ratio, consistent with the input record values. GI is estimated from the closest peer-reviewed analogous entry (white French baguette, Foster-Powell et al. 2002). Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in approved sources and the blended estimate is internally consistent.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: French bread (FDC ID 325871) and sweetened condensed milk (FDC ID 172185) blended ~60:40 by edible weight with a small butter contribution. Input record values were retained as they are broadly consistent with this estimate. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and full micronutrient profiles for this specific blended preparation are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: neither white bread nor sweetened condensed milk contains meaningful vitamin C per USDA SR Legacy data. 4. trans_fat_g of 0.33 g/100 g is retained from the input record; this is plausible given the butter component and is consistent with USDA data for butter-containing baked goods. 5. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white French baguette from Foster-Powell et al. (2002) AJCN peer-reviewed international GI table; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found. 6. Singapore HPB Foodo database consulted but no specific entry found for condensed-milk-topped French breadstick; USDA SR Legacy used as primary approved source per source policy fallback.
